title: Course Capstone: Your 280-Day Graduation!
concept: Across 280 days you built a complete real-world machine learning pipeline, from raw data to a tuned, deployed churn predictor, and every phase of this course, from foundations to MLOps, was one piece of that same system.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why did StreamBox need to keep monitoring its deployed churn model for drift, even after a successful launch?

options.text: Because real customer behavior changes over time, and a model trained on old patterns can quietly grow less accurate
Because deployed models automatically delete themselves after a few months
Because the support dashboard requires a new model file every single day regardless of accuracy

exercises.explain: Exactly — the world keeps moving after deployment, so watching for drift is how you catch a model quietly going stale.

cards.title: Look how far you've come
cards.body: 280 days ago, you started by asking "What even is AI?" Today, you personally walked a real company from a raw spreadsheet of 500,000 customers all the way to a tuned, deployed model that spots future churners — and shifted a decision threshold to save real money. That is the whole job.

cards.title: The full journey, in five acts
cards.body: You built foundations (what ML is, features, the math underneath), then learned to predict (regression and classification), then to judge your predictions honestly (evaluation metrics), then to find hidden patterns with no answer key (clustering, PCA, recommendations), and finally to choose the right tool for the job (kNN, Naive Bayes, SVMs, Trees, Ensembles, time series) — before running that entire pipeline for real, end to end.

cards.title: You built the whole skyscraper
cards.body: Day 1 was pouring the foundation. The math and evaluation units were the steel frame. Every algorithm you learned was a different tool in your belt. StreamBox's churn predictor was you using every single one of those tools, in the right order, to build something that actually stands.

cards.title: One project, your whole toolkit
cards.body: Cleaning StreamBox's messy signup_date formats used your data-cleaning skills. Encoding plan_type used categorical encoding. Choosing a Random Forest over Logistic Regression used your classic-algorithms knowledge. Fixing the 95%-vs-5% imbalance used SMOTE and class weights. Tuning max_depth used Grid Search. Turning Precision and Recall into a $367,500 savings used your evaluation skills. Saving and serving the model used MLOps. Every single unit showed up.

cards.title: You don't need to memorize every formula
cards.body: It's easy to think mastery means reciting every equation from memory. What actually matters — and what you now have — is the intuition: knowing which tool fits which problem, why a metric might be lying to you, and how to connect a model's score to a real-world outcome.

cards.title: From Day 1 to Day 280
cards.body: The shape of your entire journey:

left.text:
What is AI?
What is data?
No models yet

right.text:
Fixed real imbalance
Tuned & deployed a model
Saved a company real money

exercises.prompt: A streaming company wants to predict the exact number of minutes a customer will watch next week. Is this a regression or a classification task?

options.text: Regression — it's predicting a continuous number
Classification — it's sorting customers into categories
Neither — this isn't something machine learning can help with

exercises.explain: Predicting an exact numeric value, like minutes watched, is a classic regression task — straight from the very first phases of the course.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that scores 99% accuracy on its training data but only 60% on new, unseen data is a healthy, well-generalized model.
exercises.explain: False — that huge gap between training and unseen performance is the signature of overfitting, one of the very first failure modes you learned to watch for.

exercises.sentence: Grouping customers into hidden segments with no pre-existing labels, like a streaming service discovering natural viewer types, is called ___.
exercises.explain: Clustering is an unsupervised technique that finds natural groupings in data without any answer key, straight from your unsupervised learning unit.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best explains why StreamBox chose a Random Forest — an ensemble of many decision trees — over a single decision tree?

options.text: A forest of many trees, each seeing slightly different data, averages out individual mistakes and is far more robust than any single tree
A single decision tree is mathematically impossible to train
Random Forests don't actually use decision trees at all

exercises.explain: Ensembling many trees together, like a Random Forest does, reduces the risk of any one tree's quirks or overfitting driving the final prediction.

exercises.prompt: You've officially finished all 280 days of AIBites — from "What is AI?" on Day 1, through the math, every major algorithm family, time series, tuning, imbalance, and MLOps, to personally taking StreamBox's churn predictor from a blind 18%-recall model all the way to a tuned, deployed system that saved the business real money. What is the single biggest skill you now carry forward?

options.text: The ability to run the full machine learning pipeline end-to-end — preparing data, choosing and tuning the right model, evaluating it honestly, and connecting it to a real-world decision
Memorizing the exact mathematical formula for every algorithm you've ever seen
Knowing that one single algorithm always works best for every possible problem
None of it — none of this applies outside of a classroom

exercises.explain: That's it, exactly. 🎉 You didn't just learn isolated facts — you built the judgment to take a messy, real problem and carry it all the way to a working, valuable solution. Congratulations, graduate — you completed all 280 days of AIBites. Every unit, every lesson, every "aha" moment brought you here. Go build something real with it!
